imageCaption: A rooftop table at dawn, with scattered coffee cups, an open diagram notebook showing concentric circles and dates, and a single iridescent blue morpho butterfly perched on the edge of a pencil.
text:
The problem with understanding that your family is a living heartbeat holding reality together is that you become immediately aware of your family's heart rhythm, and whether it's beating in sync.

Aaliyah realized this at 6:47 AM when everyone simultaneously woke up despite having fallen asleep at completely different times on a rooftop in Seville.

Her eyes opened.

At the exact same moment, her father's did from the lounge chair where he'd eventually collapsed. Her mother stirred from where she'd dozed off against the metal table, still clutching her journal. Elijah sat straight up in the chair he'd claimed, as though someone had yanked his string. And Sully—who'd been sprawled across three chairs in maximum dog comfort—lifted his head and looked directly at Aaliyah with the kind of attention he usually reserved for glitch zones.

They all looked at each other.

"Did everyone else just—" Luisana started.

"Yeah," Anthony said.

"One long bark," Elijah translated, because Sully was doing the major-glitch bark and apparently announcing the arrival of synchronized waking was a temporal emergency in dog logic.

Aaliyah's notebook was still spread across the table. The diagram of concentric circles was still there. The notes about resonance and frequency and the fact that they'd accidentally become the map that broken time was using to navigate itself. But something was different about the paper.

The ink had moved.

Not shifted or smudged. Moved. The circles had rearranged themselves into something more precise—a pattern that looked almost like a rhythm, like a visual representation of a heartbeat graphed out across the page. And there were new annotations, written in a hand that looked almost like her handwriting but sloppier, like someone had traced the muscle memory but not quite got the pressure right.

"Did you write this?" her mother asked, looking at her father.

"Did you write this?" her father asked, looking at her mother.

"Did you write this?" both of them asked, looking at Aaliyah.

Aaliyah shook her head. The annotations were in blue pen, and she'd been using charcoal pencil all night. Besides, the words were things she wouldn't have written. The phrases were too precise, too specific, almost like they were coming from the outside examining the inside.

She leaned closer and read what ARIA-7 (it had to be ARIA-7, who else had access to temporal space and a bizarre interest in family handwriting?) had written in her notebook:

*Synchronous awakening indicates harmonic alignment. Your circadian rhythms are aligning with the temporal fracture cycle. You are beginning to beat in sync with broken time. This is progress. Continue to Córdoba. There is a device there that will allow you to measure your own frequency. You will need this data to understand what completion looks like.*

"A device," Anthony said. He was looking at the note with the expression of someone who'd learned that this version of reality made demands and asking what those demands were had become a necessity. "ARIA-7 is telling us there's a physical device in Córdoba that measures our frequency."

"That doesn't exist," Aaliyah said automatically, then caught herself. "No, that's wrong. Nothing exists anymore. That's the whole problem. Everything exists in multiple states. There could absolutely be a device in Córdoba. There could be a device in every city. Devices that were meant for one thing but are now perfect for measuring whether a family is beating in sync with their own broken timeline."

Her mother was already writing observations, documenting the synchronized waking and the handwriting-that-wasn't-handwriting with the focus of a journalist who understood that the universe had just given her physical evidence.

"How far is Córdoba?" Elijah asked.

"Two hours," Anthony said, pulling up Google Maps with the kind of muscle-memory travel planning that suggested he'd already looked this up in his head and was just confirming. "Maybe two and a half depending on traffic and how many glitches we hit."

"Or ten minutes if time is running the way it's been," Luisana added. "Or four hours backward. Or exist-simultaneously for an indeterminate amount of simultaneous time."

This was the new mathematics they lived in. The new calculus of distance and duration. Aaliyah was starting to get the hangover feeling that came from holding contradictory realities in her head too long, like her brain was a elastic band being stretched in two directions and eventually something was going to snap.

"We should eat before we leave," Elijah said with the absolute pragmatism of a seven-year-old who'd learned that adventure required snacks. "Sully says the next glitch is going to be loud."

"Three short barks means minor and established," Aaliyah said automatically, then noticed Sully wasn't barking. He was just looking at Elijah with what could only be described as acknowledgment. "Wait. How do you know the next glitch is going to be loud?"

"He told me," Elijah said. He said it the way you'd say "he told me to pass the salt"—like this was the most normal communication in the world. "Not in barks. In like... glitch language. The way he knows about temporal disturbances before they happen. He's getting better at explaining and I'm getting better at understanding."

The morpho butterfly appeared at the edge of the rooftop, brilliant blue against the Seville morning. It didn't land on anything. It just hovered, as though checking that they were still okay, still together, still beating at the right frequency.

Anthony gathered the empty coffee cups. Luisana closed her journal but kept it close to her chest like it contained something precious, which it did—documentation of their own hearts. Aaliyah folded her diagram notebook carefully, examining the additions ARIA-7 had made. They weren't ominous, exactly. But they were specific in a way that suggested the AI had moved from helpful observer to something more involved. Something invested.

Córdoba was two hours away. Or ten minutes. Or simultaneous-time depending on the glitch's interpretation of distance.

Somewhere in that city was a device that could measure whether they were beating right.

Aaliyah wasn't sure if she wanted to know if they were.

"Come on," her mother said, standing and extending her hand. "We should get moving. We've got a frequency to test."

As they turned to leave the rooftop, Aaliyah heard Sully bark once—long, serious, unmistakable.

Major glitch incoming.

And she realized, with the kind of certainty that came from being seventeen and having accidentally become the cartographer of her family's own heart, that the next twenty days were going to be about learning what it felt like when your love for each other wasn't metaphorical anymore.

When it became measurable.

When it became the only thing between the world and infinite buffering.

Twenty days.

One frequency.

One last chance to discover if a family's heartbeat could heal everything.
